name: Taiwan Sunflower Movement
outcome_summary: The CSSTA trade agreement was indefinitely shelved—a clear win on the immediate demand. New cross-strait oversight legislation was passed. The movement catalyzed a generation of civic activists, several of whom entered formal politics through the New Power Party. The KMT lost the 2016 election partly due to youth political awakening. However, the longer-term structural issue—Taiwan's democratic sovereignty under pressure from Beijing—remains existential and unresolved.

progress_markers.event: Ma Ying-jeou elected on cross-strait economic normalization platform; KMT begins Economic Cooperation Framework Agreement with China
ECFA (Economic Cooperation Framework Agreement) signed with China; critics warn of increasing economic dependence on Beijing
Ma Ying-jeou re-elected; youth political disillusionment growing among DPP-leaning urban youth
Cross-Strait Services Trade Agreement (CSSTA) negotiations completed; critics warn it opens sensitive service sectors to Chinese capital without adequate public debate
February-March: student groups and civic organizations form to oppose the CSSTA; Black Island Nation Youth Front mobilizes campus opposition
March 18: KMT committee chair passes CSSTA in under 60 seconds without clause-by-clause review; students storm and occupy Legislative Yuan
March 30: 500,000 march outside Legislative Yuan—largest protest in Taiwan history at that point; public support for students strong
April 6: Legislative Yuan Speaker Wang Jin-pyng promises to pass cross-strait oversight bill before reviewing CSSTA; students agree to leave
April 10: Occupation ends; CSSTA indefinitely shelved; Ma government agrees to oversight legislation requirement
Lin Fei-fan and other leaders launch civic organizations; Chen Wei-ting joins Freddy Lim's music-to-politics networks
New Power Party (NPP) founded by Sunflower leaders including Huang Kuo-chang and musician Freddy Lim
DPP wins presidential and legislative elections; NPP wins 5 legislative seats—first direct electoral conversion of movement energy
Cross-Strait Oversight Bill framework formally confirmed as legislative priority; CSSTA remains unratified
Hong Kong protests amplify Taiwan's concerns about 'one country, two systems'; Taiwan identity consciousness strengthened further
DPP wins third consecutive presidential election (Lai Ching-te); CSSTA has never been ratified; Taiwan civic engagement among youth remains high

lead_orgs.name: Black Island Nation Youth Front (黑色島國青年陣線)
lead_orgs.description: Student organization that coordinated the initial occupation of the Legislative Yuan; founded by Chen Wei-ting

lead_orgs.name: Green Citizens' Action Alliance (綠色公民行動聯盟)
lead_orgs.description: Environmental and civic NGO that joined the coalition; provided organizational logistics support

lead_orgs.name: New Power Party (時代力量)
lead_orgs.description: Political party founded by Sunflower leaders in 2015 to convert movement energy into electoral politics; won 5 legislative seats in 2016

funders: DPP-aligned donor networks for post-movement party-building
celebrities: Freddy Lim (rock musician / activist, later NPP legislator)
Various Taiwanese artists and cultural figures who supported the occupation publicly
government_champions: Wang Jin-pyng (Legislative Yuan Speaker, brokered the agreement to leave)
DPP legislators who supported students' demands

villains: Kuomintang (KMT) ruling party that pushed CSSTA through without adequate review
Pro-Beijing business interests who supported CSSTA
allies: Small and medium Taiwanese businesses opposed to CSSTA

international: US expressed quiet concern about Taiwan's economic dependence on China
Hong Kong pro-democracy groups in solidarity

name: Sri Lanka Aragalaya
outcome_summary: The Aragalaya successfully removed the Rajapaksa family from power—a historic achievement. However, the Parliament that replaced Gotabaya with Ranil Wickremesinghe (a Rajapaksa loyalist) implemented harsh IMF-mandated austerity. The new constitution that protesters demanded was never drafted. In 2024, Anura Kumara Dissanayake (NPP/JVP) won the presidency partly on the Aragalaya's legacy—an indirect but significant political shift. The economic crisis that caused the Aragalaya remains only partially resolved.

progress_markers.event: Gotabaya Rajapaksa elected on nationalist platform; implements economic mismanagement including fertilizer ban and tax cuts depleting reserves
COVID-19 destroys tourism (Sri Lanka's major forex earner); foreign reserves collapse from $7.5 billion to near zero
Overnight ban on chemical fertilizers destroys rice harvest; food prices surge; Rajapaksa family ignores expert warnings
March: Pharmacies empty of medicines; fuel queues stretch 3km; 13-hour daily power cuts; foreign debt default announced
March 31: Spontaneous gathering outside Gotabaya's private residence without party organization—'GotaGoHome' emerges
GotaGoGama protest camp outside Presidential Secretariat grows into parallel community with library, medical tent, free food, yoga—cross-ethnic, cross-religious
May 9: PM Mahinda Rajapaksa resigns after pro-government thugs attack protest camp; Wickremesinghe appointed PM
July 9: Hundreds of thousands storm Presidential Palace; Gotabaya Rajapaksa flees to Maldives then Singapore
July 14: Gotabaya Rajapaksa resigns—first president in Sri Lankan history removed by popular protest
July 20: Parliament elects Ranil Wickremesinghe as president—seen as establishment continuity candidate, not reform
July 22: Military raids GotaGoGama, forcibly removes protesters; opposition leaders arrested under PTA (Prevention of Terrorism Act)
IMF $2.9 billion bailout secured under harsh austerity conditions; electricity tariffs tripled; VAT increased; civil service wages cut
Sri Lanka settles external debt with bilateral creditors; economy stabilizes but poverty rates remain high
September: Anura Kumara Dissanayake (AKD/NPP) wins presidential election—first left-wing president in Sri Lankan history; NPP wins parliamentary supermajority in November
AKD government attempts to renegotiate IMF terms and prosecute Rajapaksa-era corruption; accountability for protest crackdown pending

lead_orgs.name: Inter University Student Federation (IUSF)
lead_orgs.description: Major student federation that provided organizational backbone for early protest phases

lead_orgs.name: National People's Power (NPP/JVP)
lead_orgs.description: Marxist-rooted party that absorbed Aragalaya energy into electoral politics; AKD won presidency in 2024

lead_orgs.name: GotaGoGama Collective
lead_orgs.description: Informal collective of civil society groups that ran the protest camp at Galle Face as a model community space

funders: Sri Lankan diaspora in Australia, UK, US, Canada (significant remittance funding for protesters)
International human rights organizations for legal support
celebrities: Various Sri Lankan artists and musicians who performed at GotaGoGama
government_champions: Anura Kumara Dissanayake (NPP opposition leader, later president)
Sajith Premadasa (SJB opposition leader)

villains: Rajapaksa family business networks
Ceylon Electricity Board (CEB) and mismanaged state enterprises
allies: Ceylon Chamber of Commerce (initially cautious, eventually supported reform call)

international: IMF (conditions controversial but provided fiscal framework)
India (emergency credit lines for fuel and medicine)

name: Thailand Student Democracy Movement
outcome_summary: The movement achieved no structural reforms. Constitutional demands were rejected; calls for monarchy reform were ruled unconstitutional by the Constitutional Court. Hundreds of protesters faced lèse-majesté prosecutions. The 2023 elections showed the movement's cultural impact—progressive Move Forward party won the most seats—but was blocked from forming government and its leader subsequently banned. Thailand's democratic regression continued under Prayuth and his successor Srettha Thavisin.

progress_markers.event: Military coup (NCPO) overthrows elected government; Prayuth Chan-o-cha seizes power; political opposition driven underground
Junta manipulates 2019 election through constitutional engineering; Prayuth 'wins'; youth anger at rigged transition intensifies
February: Future Forward Party dissolved by Constitutional Court; student 'flash mobs' erupt at universities across Thailand
March-July: COVID-19 suppresses protests temporarily; economic pain from pandemic deepens grievances
August 10: Student protest at Democracy Monument; first public call for monarchy reform in modern Thai history by Panusaya Sithijirawattanakul ('Rung') reading 10-point manifesto
Three-finger Hunger Games salute adopted across Thai protest movement; Harry Potter and Hamtaro memes used to critique monarchy without explicit lèse-majesté violations
October 14-21: Mass protests; state of emergency declared in Bangkok; water cannons with dye used against protesters at Patumwan intersection
Constitutional Court rules that monarchy reform demands constitute attempt to 'overthrow' the constitutional monarchy—essentially criminalizing the movement's core demand
Mass lèse-majesté charges filed; Arnon Nampa, Parit Chiwarak, Jatupat Boonpattararaksa among hundreds charged; many held in pretrial detention
Hunger strikes by imprisoned activists draw international attention; UN Special Rapporteurs condemn prosecutions
1,634+ charged under various laws (lèse-majesté, sedition, COVID regulations); movement activists face decade-long legal battles
May elections: Move Forward party wins most seats (151/500); shows movement's cultural/electoral impact
Move Forward blocked from forming government by military-appointed Senate; Pheu Thai forms coalition with military parties instead
Move Forward renamed People's Party after Constitutional Court dissolution; leader Pita Limjaroenrat banned from politics for 10 years
Lèse-majesté prosecutions continue; People's Party remains largest opposition; structural reforms remain blocked by constitutional design

lead_orgs.name: United Front of Thammasat and Demonstration (UFTD)
lead_orgs.description: Student coalition that organized the August 2020 Thammasat rally where monarchy reform demands were first publicly stated

lead_orgs.name: Free Youth (เยาวชนปลดแอก)
lead_orgs.description: Core youth activist network that coordinated flash mob tactics and social media organizing

lead_orgs.name: Move Forward Party / People's Party
lead_orgs.description: Progressive political party that absorbed movement's electoral energy; won most seats in 2023 then was dissolved

funders: Crowdfunding via online platforms for legal defense
Diaspora Thai communities in US, Australia, Europe
celebrities: Various Thai music artists who supported openly (at personal legal risk)
International solidarity from K-pop fan communities
government_champions: Pita Limjaroenrat (Move Forward leader)
Pannika Wanich (Thanathorn's ally)

villains: Thai military (NCPO/government forces)
Monarchy-aligned business conglomerates (CP Group connections to monarchy)
State media (MCOT, NBT) suppressing protest coverage

international: Amnesty International (designated protesters as prisoners of conscience)
US State Department (issued statements of concern)

name: Japan Anti-Nuclear Movement
outcome_summary: The movement achieved its immediate goal—all 50 reactors shut down by 2013. But the Abe government reversed course from 2015 onwards, restarting reactors despite sustained public opposition. As of 2024, approximately 12 reactors are operational with more restarts planned. The movement demonstrated that Japan's democratic culture can mobilize mass public dissent for the first time in decades, but could not ultimately override energy security imperatives when economic and geopolitical pressures (post-Ukraine energy crisis) aligned against them.

progress_markers.event: March 11: Magnitude 9.0 earthquake and tsunami triggers Fukushima Daiichi triple meltdown; 150,000 evacuated from 20km exclusion zone
March-June: Government radiation data revealed as systematically understated; independent monitoring shows exclusion zone too small; public betrayal narrative emerges
July: PM Naoto Kan orders stress tests for all nuclear plants; 35 of 54 reactors offline by end of 2011
September 19: 60,000-person anti-nuclear rally at Meiji Shrine complex—first significant political mass protest in Japan in decades
May 5: Last operating reactor (Tomari Unit 3) shut down; Japan briefly achieves 'zero nuclear'
Sayonara Genpatsu petition reaches 7.5 million signatures—largest petition in Japanese history; Nobel laureate Kenzaburo Oe leads protests
July: 200,000 Friday night protests outside PM's residence in Tokyo—largest in decades; cross-generational participation
September: DPJ government announces 'nuclear-free by 2030s' policy; new Nuclear Regulation Authority established
December: Abe LDP wins election; explicitly reverses nuclear phase-out and plans reactor restarts
Abe government includes 20-22% nuclear in energy mix by 2030 in draft energy plan; nuclear restart process begins
August: Sendai reactor restarted—first in four years; nuclear restart program begins under new NRA safety standards
2011 Kumamoto earthquakes reignite safety fears but do not halt restart program; courts occasionally block specific restarts
Russia-Ukraine war causes energy crisis; Kishida government announces acceleration of reactor restarts and potential new plant construction
GX (Green Transformation) policy approved; Japan reverses decades of post-Fukushima direction; long-term operation extensions approved
12+ reactors operational; public opposition remains high (60%+) but overridden by energy security policy; decommissioning of older reactors continues

lead_orgs.name: Sayonara Genpatsu (さようなら原発)
lead_orgs.description: Coalition that organized the 7.5 million signature petition and major Tokyo rallies; convened by intellectuals and artists

lead_orgs.name: Metropolitan Coalition Against Nukes (首都圏反原発連合)
lead_orgs.description: Organized the Friday PM residence protests; peaked at 200,000 participants in July 2012

lead_orgs.name: Citizens' Nuclear Information Center (CNIC)
lead_orgs.description: Technical expert organization providing independent radiation data that contradicted government; established 1975

funders: Some union support (minority—major unions such as Rengo were ambivalent due to nuclear worker membership)
celebrities: Haruki Murakami (gave famous Barcelona speech against nuclear power)
government_champions: Naoto Kan (PM during disaster, became anti-nuclear after leaving office)
DPJ reformers

villains: Tokyo Electric Power Company (TEPCO) - operator of Fukushima; suppressed data and mismanaged disaster
Nuclear power industry consortium
Major utilities (Kansai Electric, Kyushu Electric) pushing for restarts
allies: Renewable energy sector benefiting from nuclear phase-out period

international: IAEA (sent multiple inspection missions; recommendations largely accepted by NRA)
German government (Merkel cited Japan in accelerating German nuclear exit)

name: Pakistan Aurat March
outcome_summary: The Aurat March has not produced specific legislation but has fundamentally shifted the public discourse around women's rights in Pakistan. The annual march continues despite violent threats and legal challenges. 'Mera jism meri marzi' (My body my choice) became one of the most contested political slogans in Pakistani history. The Domestic Violence Act was passed in Sindh (2013, strengthened) and Punjab (2021) partly in this movement's wake. However, femicide rates remain high, honor killing protections remain weak, and the movement faces intensifying conservative backlash.

progress_markers.event: Women's Action Forum founded in response to Zia ul-Haq's Islamization laws; Pakistan's first organized feminist movement
Malala Yousafzai wins Nobel Peace Prize; global attention on Pakistani women's rights but limited domestic political change
Qandeel Baloch (social media star) murdered in 'honor killing' by brother; murder broadcast on social media creates national shock
Parliament passes Anti-Honor Killing law (Criminal Law Amendment Act)—first explicit criminalization of 'honor' as defense for murder
#MeToo global wave reaches Pakistan; Pakistani women begin speaking publicly about harassment in universities and workplaces
March 8: First Aurat March in Karachi, Lahore, Islamabad—International Women's Day marches explicitly framed as feminist
Second Aurat March; 'Mera jism meri marzi' poster triggers national controversy; religious groups and PTI politicians condemn march; FIRs (police complaints) filed
Backlash paradoxically amplifies movement nationally and internationally; diaspora Pakistanis join globally; march becomes annual fixture
Imran Khan government attempts to frame Aurat March as 'anti-Islamic' and 'Western-funded'; High Court petitions to ban march; courts reject bans
Punjab Domestic Violence Act passed with stronger provisions than previous law
Fifth Aurat March; largest ever; 'Inquilab aurat ka' (Women's revolution) becomes new rallying cry
Multiple court challenges to march permits; violence against some participants; Imran Khan-affiliated religious groups organize counter-marches
Aurat March continues; no major national legislation; discourse shift measurable in media coverage and political language around women's issues

lead_orgs.name: Hum Aurtein (We Women)
lead_orgs.description: Core organizing collective behind the Aurat March in Karachi; intersectional feminist framework including trans rights

lead_orgs.name: Women's Action Forum (WAF)
lead_orgs.description: Founded 1981; provided institutional memory and networking for the new generation of feminists

lead_orgs.name: Girls at Dhabas
lead_orgs.description: Feminist movement challenging public space exclusion; contributed to Aurat March visual culture

funders: Some international women's rights foundations (Ford Foundation, UN Women—though government attacked these)
government_champions: Sherry Rehman (PPP senator, women's rights advocate)
Minority voices within PTI and other parties

villains: Religious right media platforms amplifying backlash
Some major TV channels that gave disproportionate airtime to movement critics
allies: Some independent media outlets covering movement sympathetically

international: Global solidarity during COVID when march went online

name: Myanmar Civil Disobedience Movement
outcome_summary: The CDM was crushed in urban areas by sustained military violence. By mid-2021, over 1,000 protesters had been killed and thousands imprisoned. The junta maintained control of major cities. The pro-democracy resistance morphed into an armed struggle (People's Defence Force) that has achieved significant territorial control in ethnic areas by 2024-2025, but at enormous civilian cost. Myanmar is in civil war with no near-term path to democratic resolution. The CDM spirit continues in resistance zones but the non-violent movement was definitively suppressed.

progress_markers.event: Partial democratic opening under Thein Sein government; Aung San Suu Kyi released from house arrest; NLD participates in politics
NLD wins landslide election; Suu Kyi effectively leads government as State Counsellor; military retains 25% of parliamentary seats by constitutional design
November: NLD wins second landslide election; military claims fraud but election monitors find results credible
February 1 (pre-dawn): Military arrests Suu Kyi, President Win Myint, and elected government members; coup announced on state TV
February 2-5: Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM) launches; doctors, nurses, teachers, civil servants refuse to work for junta—largest professional strike in Myanmar's history
Three-finger salute (borrowed from Thai protesters) adopted; daily street protests with hundreds of thousands; international community condemns coup
February 28: Junta uses lethal force; first protesters killed; UN Special Envoy Thomas Andrews reports security forces firing on crowds
March-April: Systematic campaign of arrests, torture, killings; internet shutdowns; crackdowns intensify; protests driven underground in cities
May: People's Defence Force (PDF) formed by CRPH; armed resistance begins; ethnic armed organizations ally with pro-democracy forces
Junta executes four prominent democracy activists including Ko Jimmy and Phyo Zeya Thaw—first executions in Myanmar in decades
Civil war expands; PDF and ethnic armed groups control significant territory in Sagaing, Magway, Chin State, Kayah
Operation 1027: Brotherhood Alliance offensive seizes 200+ military outposts in Shan State; major territorial defeats for junta
Major towns in multiple states fall to resistance forces; junta territory significantly reduced; military under severe pressure
Civil war continues; junta controls Naypyidaw and major cities; NUG (National Unity Government) administers resistance areas; outcome uncertain

lead_orgs.name: Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM)
lead_orgs.description: Decentralized strikes by government workers, healthcare workers, teachers refusing to work under junta

lead_orgs.name: National Unity Government (NUG)
lead_orgs.description: Government-in-exile formed by elected MPs and civil society leaders; coordinates armed and diplomatic resistance

lead_orgs.name: People's Defence Force (PDF)
lead_orgs.description: Armed resistance force formed under NUG; grew to estimated 60,000+ fighters by 2023

funders: Myanmar diaspora worldwide (largest per capita donation to resistance in Asia)
celebrities: Aung San Suu Kyi (imprisoned; remains symbolic leader though controversial for Rohingya position)
Various Myanmar artists and musicians who fled abroad and amplified resistance
government_champions: NUG leadership (Win Myint, Mahn Win Khaing Than)

villains: Myanmar military (Tatmadaw) and its business conglomerate MEHL (Myanmar Economic Holdings Limited)
TotalEnergies, Chevron (initially continued Yadana gas pipeline payments to junta, later withdrew)
Various Chinese companies maintaining business with junta

international: ASEAN (largely ineffective; 5-point consensus ignored by junta)
US, UK, EU (sanctions of limited effect)
China (maintains relations with junta while claiming neutrality)

name: Philippines Anti-EJK / Duterte Accountability Movement
outcome_summary: The movement raised significant international awareness but failed to stop the drug war killings while Duterte was in power. The ICC opened a preliminary inquiry and then formal investigation, but Philippines withdrew from the ICC in 2019. Killings slowed after Duterte left office (constitutionally barred from re-election in 2022). Leila de Lima was imprisoned for 6 years and released in 2023. No senior official has been prosecuted domestically. Marcos Jr. continued many Duterte-era policies. Environmental defender killings remain among the world's highest.

progress_markers.event: Philippines already world's most dangerous country for environmental defenders per Global Witness data; 'red-tagging' of activists established practice under successive governments
May: Rodrigo Duterte wins presidential election on explicit extermination rhetoric; 'war on drugs' begins immediately
First months: estimated 3,000-7,000 killed in drug war operations and vigilante killings; police kill alleged drug suspects with near-total impunity
Senator Leila de Lima launches Senate investigation into drug war killings; government targets her in retaliation
February: De Lima arrested and imprisoned on drug charges widely seen as fabricated; placed in detention for 6 years
International Criminal Court opens preliminary investigation into Philippines drug war killings
Philippines withdraws from ICC Rome Statute (effective March 2019) in response to ICC investigation—first country to withdraw under investigation
ABS-CBN (Philippines' largest broadcaster) faces franchise revocation threats for critical coverage; eventually shut down 2020
Maria Ressa (Rappler CEO, documenting drug war and disinformation) convicted of cyber-libel; international press freedom outcry
Maria Ressa wins Nobel Peace Prize—international recognition of Philippines press freedom crisis
ICC Prosecutor Bensouda determines drug war crimes may constitute crimes against humanity; formal investigation authorized
Duterte constitutionally barred from re-election; killings drop significantly under Marcos Jr.; de Lima's legal situation improves
February: Leila de Lima released from detention; conviction overturned by Supreme Court
ICC investigation continues; Duterte arrested in 2025 and surrendered to ICC—historic development; domestic impunity for most killings continues

lead_orgs.name: Rappler
lead_orgs.description: Digital media company led by Maria Ressa that systematically documented drug war killings and disinformation; Nobel Peace Prize 2021

lead_orgs.name: Commission on Human Rights Philippines
lead_orgs.description: Government body under Chito Gascon that documented killings despite administration pressure; defunded by Duterte-ally Congress

lead_orgs.name: Campaign Against the Return of the Marcoses and Martial Law (CARMMA)
lead_orgs.description: Coalition of human rights groups documenting EJKs and fighting accountability

government_champions: Leila de Lima (imprisoned opposition senator)
Leni Robredo (VP, actively documented killings)

villains: Duterte administration and Philippine National Police (PNP)
Facebook/Meta (algorithms amplified pro-Duterte disinformation network)

international: EU (removed GSP+ trade preferences over human rights)

name: Indonesia Anti-Corruption KPK Defense
outcome_summary: Students partially succeeded in withdrawing some of the September 2019 legislation package but failed on the most important piece: the KPK revision. The new KPK supervisory board effectively hobbled the body's independence. KPK conviction rates dropped dramatically post-2020. Indonesia's anti-corruption gains from 2003-2019 were significantly reversed. Jokowi's administration continued despite weakened oversight. The movement demonstrated the vulnerability of independent institutions to rapid legislative assault.

progress_markers.event: KPK (Corruption Eradication Commission) established by post-Reformasi legislation; becomes most trusted institution in Indonesia
KPK faces first political attack ('Cicak vs. Buaya' — gecko vs. crocodile) when police attempt to frame KPK leaders; public backlash saves KPK
Jokowi first term: KPK prosecutes high-profile politicians including from PDI-P (Jokowi's own party); tensions grow between KPK and political establishment
September: Parliament moves to revise KPK law during brief recess before new parliament takes office; rushed through without public consultation
September 23-24: Student protests at parliament in Senayan; hundreds of thousands mobilize across 20+ cities; police use tear gas, rubber bullets; 2 students killed
Government withdraws several bills from legislative package under pressure (Criminal Code revisions, Land Bill, Correctional Institution Bill)
KPK revision law passes despite protests; new supervisory board (Dewan Pengawas) installed; KPK employees must re-qualify as civil servants—process removes dozens of experienced investigators
Under new KPK structure: conviction rate drops from 100% to around 96%; investigation pace slows; high-profile politicians increasingly escape scrutiny
KPK employee ethics committee clears almost all candidates in competency tests after firing 56 'compromised' employees—widely seen as gutting institutional capacity
KPK head Firli Bahuri (installed under new governance structure) faces multiple ethics complaints; investigation of minister leads to bribery accusation against Firli himself
Firli Bahuri suspended and named as bribery suspect—shows KPK not entirely dead—but damage to institutional independence is severe
Prabowo Subianto (Jokowi son-in-law's father-in-law) becomes president; KPK reform prospects uncertain; Indonesia's Corruption Perceptions Index has worsened

lead_orgs.name: BEM (Badan Eksekutif Mahasiswa)
lead_orgs.description: Student executive bodies across major Indonesian universities; coordinated national protest mobilization

lead_orgs.name: Indonesia Corruption Watch (ICW)
lead_orgs.description: Leading anti-corruption NGO providing documentation and legal analysis; key voice against KPK revisions

lead_orgs.name: YLBHI (Indonesian Legal Aid Foundation)
lead_orgs.description: Provided legal support for arrested protesters and filed constitutional challenges

funders: Foundations including Ford Foundation, Transparency International
celebrities: Various Indonesian artists and musicians who expressed support
Former KPK leaders Abraham Samad and Bambang Widjojanto
government_champions: Some PDI-P minority voices
Transparency-focused municipal politicians

villains: Established political parties (Golkar, PDI-P, Gerindra) that pushed KPK revisions
Oligarch-connected businesses threatened by KPK investigations

name: Bangladesh Garment Workers / Rana Plaza
outcome_summary: Rana Plaza produced the Bangladesh Accord on Fire and Building Safety—the first legally binding international corporate accountability agreement in garment supply chains. Over 200 brands signed. Factory inspections improved measurably. However, minimum wages remained inadequate ($95/month by 2024 vs. $177 demanded by workers). The Accord's original 5-year mandate required repeated renewal campaigns. Sohel Rana was convicted in 2023 after a decade-long trial. Structural power imbalance between brands and workers persists; Bangladesh brands still compete primarily on price, not safety.

progress_markers.event: Clean Clothes Campaign, Worker Rights Consortium, and other labor rights groups begin systematic documentation of Bangladesh garment industry conditions
Bangladesh garment workers' minimum wage increased to $43/month after major strikes; brands resist pressure for supply chain accountability
November 24: Tazreen Fashions fire kills 117 workers; Walmart, Sears labels found in rubble; brands pledge action but don't implement
December: Meeting of major brands declines to sign legally binding fire and building safety accord; later described by ILRF as missed prevention opportunity
April 24, 8:45am: Rana Plaza collapses; 1,134 killed—workers had been sent in despite visible cracks the day before; trapped for days in rubble
May 13: Bangladesh Accord on Fire and Building Safety launched; legally binding; first brand signatories within days
June: Over 100 brands sign the Accord; subsequent months over 200 brands sign; ILO involvement; Worker Safety inspectors granted independent access
July: Rana Plaza Donor Trust Fund established; owner Sohel Rana arrested; Bangladesh government launches criminal investigation
Minimum wage increased to $68/month—still far below living wage but largest increase in history; 100,000+ safety hazards remediated in Accord factories
Bangladesh Accord transitions to RMG Sustainability Council; original 5-year term ends; advocacy needed for renewal
International Accord on Health and Safety signed—successor to Bangladesh Accord with expanded country scope
March: Sohel Rana convicted of negligence; sentenced to 3 years imprisonment after decade-long trial
Minimum wage increased to $95/month after major strikes (workers demanded $208); International Accord continues; EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive references Accord model

lead_orgs.name: Clean Clothes Campaign (CCC)
lead_orgs.description: International labor rights network coordinating brand accountability pressure; key drafter of the Accord

lead_orgs.name: Worker Rights Consortium (WRC)
lead_orgs.description: US-based labor rights group providing factory monitoring; participated in Accord development and enforcement

lead_orgs.name: IndustriALL Global Union / UNI Global Union
lead_orgs.description: Global union federations that signed the Bangladesh Accord as legally binding counterparties to brands

funders: Some foundation funding for NGO coordination
celebrities: Various Western fashion industry voices who expressed concern
government_champions: Bangladesh Minister of Labour (agreed to some reforms)
EU Trade Commissioner (used GSP+ conditionality)

villains: Primark, Walmart, Gap (delayed or refused Accord signing initially)
Sohel Rana and factory owners who sent workers in despite visible cracks
Building inspection authorities who were complicit
allies: PVH Corp (signed and advocated others to join)

international: OECD (due diligence guidelines referenced Rana Plaza)

name: Fridays for Future Global South Expansion
outcome_summary: The Global South expansion of FFF produced a measurable shift in the movement's racial and geographic politics—Vanessa Nakate, Mitzi Jonelle Tan, Ayisha Siddiqi, and others became central international voices rather than peripheral ones. The AP photo-cropping incident paradoxically accelerated this change. Loss-and-damage language entered UNFCCC negotiations more forcefully. However, no specific policy wins can be attributed to Global South FFF specifically; the movement remains better at cultural influence than policy conversion.

progress_markers.event: Fridays for Future begins with Thunberg; largely Global North-framed; Global South activists note the gap between who faces climate harm and who leads the movement
Vanessa Nakate begins FFF strikes in Kampala, Uganda; Mitzi Jonelle Tan in Philippines; Ayisha Siddiqi in Pakistan; 'MAPA' (Most Affected People and Areas) framing develops
September 20: Global Climate Strike—6 million participants; Global South activists prominent but still not centered in international media coverage
January 24: Associated Press wire photo from Davos shows 5 climate activists—4 white Europeans included, Vanessa Nakate cropped out; Nakate's video response goes globally viral
AP photo-cropping incident triggers global media coverage of climate movement's racial politics; 'whose climate crisis is it?' debate reshapes FFF's public identity
Nakate's response: emotionally honest video distinguishing personal pain from structural issue; establishes her as global spokesperson with unique credibility
Nakate founds Rise Up Movement (Uganda) focusing on African climate activism; Amplify Her (later Vash Green Schools) supports African youth climate education
Nakate publishes 'A Bigger Picture' (2021); Global South climate activists become permanent fixtures at UN COP negotiations rather than occasional voices
COP26 Glasgow: Loss and damage financing discussed more prominently than ever before; Santiago Network established; Global South negotiating bloc strengthened
COP27 Sharm el-Sheikh: Loss and damage fund formally established—first time wealthy nations acknowledge financial responsibility for climate harm; historic normative shift
COP28 Dubai: Loss and damage fund operationalized with initial $660 million (widely seen as inadequate but precedent-setting); Global South activists continue pressure for adequate scale

lead_orgs.name: Rise Up Movement (Uganda)
lead_orgs.description: Founded by Vanessa Nakate; amplifies African youth climate voices and supports climate education in Uganda

lead_orgs.name: Fridays for Future MAPA
lead_orgs.description: Most Affected People and Areas network within FFF; coordinates Global South activists and centers their demands in climate negotiations

lead_orgs.name: Youth Advocates for Climate Action Philippines (YACAP)
lead_orgs.description: Led by Mitzi Jonelle Tan; connects climate justice to human rights and frontline community perspectives

funders: Some European climate foundations supporting Global South youth voices
government_champions: Vanuatu, Tuvalu, Fiji and other small island states at UNFCCC
Kenya, Ethiopia, Nigeria progressive climate negotiators

villains: Major fossil fuel companies and petrostate bloc blocking loss and damage funding
Associated Press (original photo crop—later apologized)

international: UN Secretary-General (expressed strong support for youth climate activists)
IPCC (provided scientific backing for loss-and-damage claims)

name: Bolivia Cochabamba Water Wars
outcome_summary: The Water Wars achieved their immediate demand completely—Bechtel/Aguas del Tunari expelled and water privatization reversed. Oscar Olivera became a global symbol. The movement influenced the UN's 2010 recognition of water as a human right. It catalyzed Bolivia's broader leftward turn, directly enabling Evo Morales's 2005 election. Bolivia enshrined rights of nature in its 2009 constitution. The Cochabamba water system remains publicly managed as of 2024. This is one of the cleanest wins in the dataset.

progress_markers.event: World Bank conditions loans on Cochabamba water privatization; Bolivian government begins privatization process; communities begin organizing opposition
November: Law 2029 passed granting 40-year water concession to Aguas del Tunari (Bechtel subsidiary); law even prohibits collecting rainwater
January: Water bills arrive showing 200-300% increases; immediate protests begin; Coordinadora de Defensa del Agua y de la Vida formed
February-March: Escalating strikes and road blockades; government promises talks but doesn't deliver; water declared human right by Coordinadora
April 4-8: Coordinadora calls general strike; transporters block roads; police and military clash with protesters; state of siege declared; Victor Hugo Daza (17) killed by police
April 10: Government negotiates directly with Coordinadora; agrees to cancel Aguas del Tunari contract and repeal Law 2029
April 11: Aguas del Tunari expelled; SEMAPA (public water utility) restored; international anti-globalization movement celebrates as model victory
Bechtel files $50 million ICSID arbitration claim against Bolivia; international solidarity campaign mounts; Bechtel withdraws claim for nominal compensation
Bolivia Gas Wars; similar popular uprising defeats natural gas privatization; Gonzalo Sánchez de Lozada forced to resign
Evo Morales (Aymara coca farmers' leader, ally of Coordinadora) wins presidential election—Water Wars was the organizational seedbed for Bolivia's political revolution
Bolivia's new constitution under Morales enshrines rights of nature (Pachamama) and right to water as human right
UN General Assembly adopts resolution recognizing right to water—explicitly citing Bolivia's advocacy; Bolivia presented resolution from Cochabamba climate conference

lead_orgs.name: Coordinadora de Defensa del Agua y de la Vida
lead_orgs.description: Coalition of farmers, workers, and civic groups led by Oscar Olivera; organized the Water Wars resistance

lead_orgs.name: Federación de Regantes (Irrigators' Federation)
lead_orgs.description: Rural water users organization that provided community mobilization backbone outside the city

lead_orgs.name: COB (Central Obrera Boliviana - national labor union)
lead_orgs.description: National union federation that provided labor infrastructure for general strike

celebrities: Oscar Olivera (factory worker turned global icon)
government_champions: Evo Morales (MAS leader, ally)
Hugo Banzer initially agreed to cancel contract (ironically, the same dictator who privatized in first place)

villains: Bechtel Corporation / Aguas del Tunari
World Bank (mandated privatization as loan condition)
IMF (structural adjustment framework)

name: Ecuador Rights of Nature / Pacha Mama Constitution
outcome_summary: Ecuador created the world's first constitutional rights of nature framework—a genuine breakthrough in legal philosophy. Multiple subsequent court rulings have invoked Rights of Nature (most notably the 2022 Amazon case ruling against oil extraction in Yasuni-ITT, upheld by referendum in 2023). However, the Correa government that passed the constitution simultaneously expanded oil and mining extraction, demonstrating that constitutional rights without enforcement culture are limited. The framework has traveled globally—Bolivia, multiple US cities, New Zealand river personhood—making its normative impact larger than Ecuador's domestic record suggests.

progress_markers.event: CONAIE (indigenous confederation) 'levantamiento' (uprising) establishes indigenous social movements as major political force in Ecuador
Community Rights movement globally, led by Thomas Berry's 'Earth Jurisprudence'; CELDF (Community Environmental Legal Defense Fund) begins drafting rights of nature ordinances in US
Rafael Correa wins presidential election on anti-neoliberal platform; promises new constitution via Citizens' Assembly
Constituent Assembly convened; CONAIE and civil society organizations lobby for Rights of Nature provisions; Cormac Cullinan and CELDF provide legal drafting expertise
Ecuadorian Constitution adopted (September 28) with Articles 71-74 granting Nature legal rights—world first; 64% vote in favor
Bolivia's new constitution also enshrines Pachamama rights; global Earth Jurisprudence movement gains momentum
Correa government approves massive oil and mining expansion, including in sensitive Amazon areas; Rights of Nature provisions begin to show enforcement gap
First Rights of Nature case in Ecuador: Vilcabamba River wins injunction against road construction project—first Rights of Nature court victory globally
Correa leaves office with rights of nature largely unenforced for large-scale industrial projects; succeeding Moreno government continues extractive orientation
Rights of Nature adopted by Toledo, Ohio (US) for Lake Erie; framework spreads globally to cities, regions
Constitutional Court ruling: Los Cedros cloud forest protected as Rights of Nature case against mining—significant enforcement precedent
Constitutional Court rules Yasuni-ITT Amazon oil extraction violates Rights of Nature; upheld by national referendum in August 2023
Yasuni oil extraction ceasing under court order; Ecuador facing economic pressure from lost oil revenue; rights of nature framework increasingly cited in global climate litigation

lead_orgs.name: CONAIE (Confederation of Indigenous Nationalities of Ecuador)
lead_orgs.description: Most powerful indigenous confederation; lobbied strongly for Rights of Nature provisions and provided cosmovisión framework

lead_orgs.name: Community Environmental Legal Defense Fund (CELDF)
lead_orgs.description: US NGO that provided legal drafting expertise for Rights of Nature articles; Thomas Linzey key drafter

lead_orgs.name: Alianza por los Derechos de la Naturaleza (Rights of Nature Alliance)
lead_orgs.description: Ecuadorian civil society coalition that advocates for Rights of Nature enforcement post-constitution

funders: Correa government's Citizen Revolution funding for constitutional process
government_champions: Rafael Correa (president, initially)
Alberto Acosta (Constituent Assembly)

villains: Chevron/Texaco (historic oil contamination in Amazon)
OCP Ecuador and Petroecuador (state oil extraction continuing despite constitutional rights)
Mining companies operating in cloud forest areas

international: IUCN (International Union for Conservation of Nature)
Global Rights of Nature movement (Earth Law Center, Global Alliance for Rights of Nature)
UN Special Rapporteur on human rights and environment

name: Mexico Ayotzinapa 43
outcome_summary: The Ayotzinapa case produced significant political consequences—it permanently discredited Peña Nieto and contributed to AMLO's 2018 election—but failed at its core demand: finding the 43 students and bringing perpetrators to justice. As of 2025, most students remain unaccounted for. Mexico's 'historical truth' narrative was definitively discredited by the GIEI independent expert group. High-level convictions remain elusive; the Guerrero cartel-state nexus that enabled the disappearances has been partially disrupted but not dismantled.

progress_markers.event: Calderón launches 'drug war'; forced disappearances become systematic; human rights organizations document pattern nationally
Javier Sicilia 'Movement for Peace with Justice and Dignity' follows murder of his son; largest anti-violence marches in Mexican history—briefly
Night of September 26-27: Police in Iguala attack Ayotzinapa student buses; 43 students disappear; 6 killed; mass graves found in following days
'43 = todos' slogan; monthly parents' marches begin; specific number makes abstraction impossible; faces of 43 plastered across Mexico
International attention: global protests in diaspora cities; EZLN solidarity; university shutdowns across Mexico
PGR's 'historical truth' (students burned at Cocula dump) demolished by GIEI independent expert group using forensic evidence; narrative accountability achieved
Peña Nieto government's credibility permanently destroyed; 'Es la Estado!' (It's the State!) framing entered mainstream discourse
GIEI expelled from Mexico after government refuses to give access to military information; investigation stalled
AMLO wins presidential election partly on anti-impunity/Ayotzinapa platform; creates Undersecretariat for Human Rights for Alejandro Encinas
New official investigation (GTJP) names military officers as complicit; 'state crime' formally acknowledged by AMLO government
Arrests of military personnel; but cases face legal obstacles; military resists accountability; AMLO himself protective of military despite findings
AMLO's defense of military limits accountability; some arrests overturned on procedural grounds; parents still march monthly
Most students still not found; investigation ongoing but politically constrained; parents' movement continues as permanent moral witness; case shapes anti-impunity campaigns globally

lead_orgs.name: Families of the 43 (Padres de Familia)
lead_orgs.description: Parents who became the moral core of the movement; monthly marches sustained for years; unimpeachable moral authority

lead_orgs.name: GIEI (Interdisciplinary Group of Independent Experts)
lead_orgs.description: IACHR-mandated independent expert group that demolished the government's 'historical truth' narrative using forensic evidence

lead_orgs.name: Tlachinollan Human Rights Center
lead_orgs.description: Guerrero-based human rights organization that has represented Ayotzinapa families from the beginning; Vidulfo Rosales is lead attorney

celebrities: Various Mexican artists and musicians who joined marches
government_champions: Alejandro Encinas (AMLO's human rights undersecretary; led new investigation)
IACHR (provided institutional legitimacy)

villains: Guerreros Unidos cartel
Iguala municipal police and government (Jose Luis Abarca, then-mayor)
Elements of Federal Police and military with documented collusion

name: Guatemala Anti-Corruption CICIG Protests
outcome_summary: The 2015 anti-corruption protests achieved the remarkable goal of forcing a sitting president to resign—unprecedented in Guatemala. CICIG's existence as an international validator with real prosecutorial power was the decisive factor. However, the 2019 expulsion of CICIG under US/Trump pressure reversed institutional gains. Subsequent elections have been contested; Bernardo Arévalo won the 2023 presidential election on anti-corruption platform and survived a coup attempt against his inauguration—showing the movement's lasting effects on political culture even as institutions have weakened.

progress_markers.event: CICIG (UN-mandated anti-corruption commission with prosecutorial powers) established under Oscar Berger government—unique international accountability mechanism
CICIG under Castresana begins prosecuting police-crime nexus; establishes credibility as genuinely independent institution
#RenunciaYa (Resign Now) hashtag begins circulating as corruption scandals accumulate; CICIG investigations generate evidence
April 16: CICIG press conference reveals 'La Línea'—systematic customs fraud scheme directly linked to VP Baldetti and subsequently President Pérez Molina
May: VP Roxana Baldetti resigns; arrested for corruption
August-September: Weekly '#SábadosDeProtesta' in Guatemala City; up to 100,000 march with rare cross-class and cross-ethnic coalition
September 3: Otto Pérez Molina resigns as president—two days before election; arrested September 8; unprecedented constitutional accountability
CICIG continues investigations; new president Jimmy Morales elected on anti-corruption platform (irony: later himself target of CICIG)
President Morales attempts to expel CICIG after it investigates him for illegal campaign financing; Constitutional Court blocks expulsion
Morales (with US Trump administration acquiescence) refuses to renew CICIG mandate; CICIG leaves Guatemala September 2019—major institutional loss
Bernardo Arévalo wins presidential election on anti-corruption platform; establishment attempts to block his inauguration through prosecutors and courts
Arévalo inaugurated despite coup attempt; reformist government faces entrenched corrupt establishment without CICIG's institutional support
Arévalo government struggling against 'pacto de corruptos' (corruption pact); shows movement's cultural legacy but limits without institutional validator

lead_orgs.name: CICIG (International Commission Against Impunity in Guatemala)
lead_orgs.description: UN-backed commission with prosecutorial capacity; provided the institutional legitimacy that converted accusations into criminal charges

lead_orgs.name: Plataforma Nacional para la Reforma del Estado
lead_orgs.description: Civil society coalition that organized #SábadosDeProtesta and provided movement coordination

lead_orgs.name: CIDH / IACHR
lead_orgs.description: Provided international human rights monitoring and reporting that supported CICIG's legitimacy

funders: CICIG funded by Sweden, Netherlands, Canada, US (under Obama)
European Union development funds for civil society
celebrities: Claudia Paz y Paz (former Attorney General, prosecuted Ríos Montt for genocide)
Thelma Aldana (Attorney General who worked with CICIG, later presidential candidate)
government_champions: Ivan Velasquez (CICIG Director, Colombian prosecutor)
Constitutional Court that blocked Morales's expulsion attempt

villains: La Línea customs fraud network (politicians and business elite)
CACIF (business elites—Guatemala's Chamber of Commerce)
allies: Some mid-size business sectors opposed to corruption as competitive barrier

international: US State Department (under Obama supportive; under Trump reversed)

name: Peru Anti-Boluarte Protests
outcome_summary: Protests demanding new elections and Boluarte's resignation achieved neither outcome. Boluarte remained in power as of 2025 despite calls for resignation. At least 49 protesters were killed by security forces, with deaths concentrated in indigenous Andean regions. The Defensoría del Pueblo (Ombudsman) documented human rights violations. No accountability for deaths. Castillo faces ongoing corruption trials. The protests successfully demonstrated the depth of Lima-vs-regions structural divide but produced no structural change.

progress_markers.event: PPK elected; Peru begins 5-president-in-5-years cycle of political instability driven by congressional opposition and corruption
June: Pedro Castillo (rural teacher, union leader from Cajamarca) wins presidential election by narrow margin—first indigenous Andean president; establishment resists from day one
Castillo government faces six cabinet reshuffles in 16 months; Congress initiates two impeachment proceedings; government paralyzed by coalition dysfunction
December 7: Castillo attempts self-coup (dissolve Congress, rule by emergency decree); Congress impeaches him within hours; Boluarte (VP) assumes presidency
December 12 onwards: Mass protests demanding Boluarte resign and new elections; concentrated in southern indigenous regions (Ayacucho, Puno, Cusco)
December 15: Ayacucho massacre—10 protesters killed by military; single worst day; Boluarte government declared state of emergency in multiple regions
January-February: Ongoing protests; road blockades including Machu Picchu; total death toll reaches 49; international tourism disrupted
Congress repeatedly refuses early elections; Boluarte survives despite approval ratings below 15%; protests lose momentum through attrition and repression
Inter-American Commission on Human Rights condemns killings; Defensoría del Pueblo documents violations; some accountability cases opened but slow
Boluarte still in office; Castillo's trial continues; 2026 elections approaching; Andean communities' structural exclusion unchanged; impunity for protest deaths continues

lead_orgs.name: CGTP (General Confederation of Peruvian Workers)
lead_orgs.description: National union federation that supported protests and called strikes

lead_orgs.name: Asociaciones de Comunidades Campesinas y Nativas (peasant and native community associations)
lead_orgs.description: Community organizations in Puno, Ayacucho, Cusco that organized and sustained protests in the south

lead_orgs.name: Coordinadora Nacional de Derechos Humanos
lead_orgs.description: Human rights coalition documenting deaths and demanding accountability

funders: International solidarity from indigenous rights networks
celebrities: Various Peruvian artists who expressed solidarity
government_champions: Pedro Castillo (imprisoned, still contested symbol of indigenous representation)
Defensoría del Pueblo (Eliana Revollar, criticized government)

villains: Mining companies (extraction model represents structural issue)
Lima media oligarchy that framed protests as terrorism

name: Honduras Berta Cáceres / COPINH Anti-Dam Movement
outcome_summary: The Agua Zarca dam was canceled in November 2016—a clear, specific win. German company Voith Hydro withdrew, followed by Dutch FMO and Finnish FINNFUND. DESA (dam company) executives were convicted for Cáceres's murder in 2021. Berta Cáceres's assassination paradoxically amplified the movement globally, demonstrating that the murder of Goldman Prize winners creates international accountability pressure. However, Honduras remains the world's most dangerous country for environmental defenders, and the root causes—military-corporate alliance, weak rule of law—remain unchanged.

progress_markers.event: Berta Cáceres and COPINH begin organizing Lenca indigenous communities against planned hydroelectric projects on the Gualcarque River (sacred to Lenca people)
Agua Zarca dam concession granted to DESA without FPIC (Free, Prior, Informed Consent) consultation with Lenca communities—violating ILO Convention 169
Post-2009 Honduras coup context: extractive projects accelerate; environmental defenders increasingly targeted; murders documented by FIAN and Global Witness
COPINH organizes sustained community blockade of Agua Zarca dam construction; German company Voith Hydro, Dutch FMO, Finnish FINNFUND are funders—international pressure points identified
April: Berta Cáceres wins Goldman Environmental Prize—maximum international visibility; becomes globally protected figure theoretically
International financial institutions begin receiving pressure from human rights organizations over funding for project despite ongoing violence against COPINH
March 3: Berta Cáceres assassinated in her home in La Esperanza—seven bullets; international shock at murder of Goldman Prize winner
#JusticiaParaBerta goes globally viral; #BertaVive; international calls for investigation and project cancellation; investor withdrawal pressure intensifies
June: Voith Hydro (Germany) withdraws from Agua Zarca citing 'social conflicts'
October: FMO (Netherlands) and FINNFUND (Finland) withdraw financial support
November: Agua Zarca dam officially canceled—COPINH's decade-long campaign wins
DESA manager David Castillo arrested and eventually convicted as co-conspirator in murder; Cáceres family continues international advocacy
Seven DESA company officials convicted for Cáceres murder; corporate accountability for environmental defender murder unprecedented in Honduras
Honduras remains world's most dangerous country per capita for environmental defenders; COPINH continues; structural conditions unchanged

lead_orgs.name: COPINH (Council of Popular and Indigenous Organizations of Honduras)
lead_orgs.description: Founded by Berta Cáceres and others in 1993; represents Lenca, Tolupan, and other indigenous communities against extractive projects

lead_orgs.name: FIAN International
lead_orgs.description: Human rights organization that documented COPINH repression and filed international human rights complaints

lead_orgs.name: Global Witness
lead_orgs.description: Published annual data on environmental defender killings naming Honduras as world's most dangerous country per capita

funders: European development agencies (ironically FMO funded the dam initially)
government_champions: German, Dutch, Finnish parliamentarians who pressured their national development banks

villains: DESA (Desarrollos Energéticos SA)
Voith Hydro (German company that provided technology)
FMO (Dutch development bank)
FINNFUND (Finnish development bank)
Honduran military involved in intimidation

international: ILO Convention 169 (violated, cited repeatedly in advocacy)
UN Special Rapporteur on Human Rights Defenders

name: Haiti PetroCaribe Anti-Corruption Movement
outcome_summary: The movement correctly identified the systematic theft of PetroCaribe funds and created massive political pressure, but produced no accountability and no structural change. President Moïse survived both protest waves. He was assassinated in July 2021 in an unrelated plot. Haiti descended into gang-controlled chaos by 2022-2024, with an estimated 80% of Port-au-Prince controlled by gangs. The movement diagnosed the problem accurately but accelerated instability without building institutional alternatives, contributing to state collapse.

progress_markers.event: PetroCaribe agreement with Venezuela: Haiti can buy oil on deferred payment terms and invest the price difference; billions in development fund accumulates
Haiti's Senate begins investigation into PetroCaribe fund management; preliminary evidence of systematic diversion; no accountability results
Court of Accounts report reveals $2 billion in PetroCaribe funds diverted or missing; names former presidents Préval, Martelly, and ministers
July 6: Government raises fuel prices 51% overnight under IMF pressure; violent protests immediately; fuel price increase reversed within 48 hours by government retreat
#PetroCaribeChallenge: Haitians post ironic social media content asking 'where did the money go?' next to dilapidated infrastructure
February 2019: Prime Minister Jack Guy Lafontant resigns under protest pressure; small accountability step
October: Senate commission releases full report naming former PM Laurent Lamothe and others; street protests demand Moïse resign
Peyi lok (country lockdown): protesters blockade roads for weeks; economy collapses; Moïse refuses to resign; parliament suspended
COVID-19 suppresses street protests; Moïse governs by decree without parliament; rule-of-law erosion accelerates
July 7: Moïse assassinated by mercenaries; country descends into deeper chaos; no accountability for PetroCaribe theft
Gang federation G9 controls 80%+ of Port-au-Prince; state institutions collapse; humanitarian crisis reaches catastrophic levels
Kenya-led multinational security force arrives; PM Garry Conille resigns; Haiti remains failed state; PetroCaribe theft never prosecuted

lead_orgs.name: #PetroCaribeChallenge social media collective
lead_orgs.description: Decentralized digital movement; no formal leadership; created viral accountability demand

lead_orgs.name: PAPDA (Haitian Platform to Advocate Alternative Development)
lead_orgs.description: Development advocacy NGO that provided economic analysis of PetroCaribe mismanagement

lead_orgs.name: Haitian progressive churches
lead_orgs.description: Some progressive Catholic and Protestant congregations that framed corruption in moral terms

celebrities: Various diaspora Haitian artists and musicians in Miami, New York, Montreal who amplified internationally
government_champions: Some senators who led PetroCaribe investigation

villains: Digicel (Sean Combs-associated company received PetroCaribe contracts amid corruption allegations)
Various construction companies that received inflated contracts

international: US (sanctioned some officials but limited leverage)

name: Brazil Amazon Defenders / Yanomami Crisis
outcome_summary: Lula's government produced measurable results: Amazon deforestation reduced 50% in 2023 vs. Bolsonaro-era peak; Yanomami humanitarian emergency declared and military deployed; killers of Bruno Pereira and Dom Phillips convicted. However, illegal mining expulsions are incomplete; garimpeiro operations have partially resumed; broader extractive pressures persist. The structural contradiction—Brazil's economy depends partly on Amazon extraction—remains unresolved. The environmental recovery is real but fragile.

progress_markers.event: Bolsonaro takes office; IBAMA (environmental enforcement) gutted; FUNAI (indigenous affairs) dismantled; Amazon deforestation jumps 80% in first year
Environmental advocates, indigenous leaders, and journalists face criminalization; 'red-tagging' of Amazon defenders increases
COVID spreads rapidly through indigenous communities (especially Yanomami); federal government ignores crisis; illegal miners flood into Yanomami territory
Amazon deforestation reaches highest level since 2008; deforestation under Bolsonaro cumulatively devastating; international pressure builds
June 5: Indigenous expert Bruno Pereira and British journalist Dom Phillips assassinated in Javari Valley; international shock; case becomes global symbol of Amazon impunity
Killers of Pereira and Phillips arrested; case breaks through international media silence on Amazon defender murders
January 1: Lula inaugurated; immediately declares Yanomami humanitarian emergency; shocking images of malnutrition and disease released (suppressed under Bolsonaro)
IBAMA, ICMBio, FUNAI reactivated with full staff; military deployed to expel illegal miners from Yanomami territory; Amazon deforestation drops 50%
Killers of Bruno Pereira convicted; Bolsonaro faces multiple criminal investigations including for inciting violence against indigenous people
Brazil leads Global Alliance Against Deforestation (GAAD); Amazon Fund reactivated (Norway, Germany contribute $1 billion+); International support for forest protection restored
Illegal gold mining partially resumes; some expelled garimpeiros return; budget constraints limit IBAMA enforcement capacity; infrastructure projects (roads, hydropower) continue
COP30 in Belém (November 2025) creates global pressure point; Lula government's environmental record mixed but substantially better than predecessor

lead_orgs.name: APIB (Articulation of Indigenous Peoples of Brazil)
lead_orgs.description: National indigenous federation led by Sonia Guajajara (later Minister of Indigenous Peoples); key advocacy and legal voice

lead_orgs.name: Hutukara Yanomami Association
lead_orgs.description: Yanomami indigenous organization led by Davi Kopenawa; documented crisis and demanded federal action

lead_orgs.name: Instituto Socioambiental (ISA)
lead_orgs.description: Major environmental and indigenous rights NGO providing documentation, mapping, and policy advocacy

celebrities: Davi Kopenawa Yanomami (global indigenous rights icon)
government_champions: Lula da Silva (president from January 2023)
Marina Silva (Environment Minister, returned for third term)
Sonia Guajajara (Minister of Indigenous Peoples)

villains: Illegal gold mining (garimpeiro) networks with political connections
Agricultural expansion lobby (bancada ruralista in Congress)
Timber and cattle ranching interests
allies: Some European retail companies committing to deforestation-free supply chains

name: Tunisia Arab Spring
outcome_summary: Tunisia achieved the only genuine democratic transition from the Arab Spring—Ben Ali fled, elections were held, a new constitution passed (2014), and the National Dialogue Quartet won the Nobel Peace Prize. However, President Kais Saied conducted a 'soft coup' in July 2021, suspended parliament, and subsequently installed a presidential system through a new constitution (2022) with vastly expanded executive power. Tunisia's democratic experiment lasted roughly 7 years before reverting to soft authoritarianism—making the final score genuinely contested.

progress_markers.event: Gafsa mining basin protests (largely ignored internationally) show deep resentment at Ben Ali's economic exclusion of interior regions; brutally suppressed
WikiLeaks cables confirm Ben Ali family corruption to Tunisian public—corroborating what everyone knew; rage over economic conditions building
December 17: Mohamed Bouazizi self-immolates in Sidi Bouzid after police confiscate his vegetable cart; protests begin in interior immediately
January 4: Bouazizi dies; protests intensify; spread to coastal cities
January 14: Ben Ali flees to Saudi Arabia after 23 years in power; 28 days after Bouazizi's self-immolation—fastest any Arab dictator had fallen
October: Free elections; Ennahda (Islamist party) wins plurality; secular fears but democratic process respected—historic
Political assassinations (Chokri Belaid, Mohamed Brahmi); Islamist-secular standoff threatens democratic transition; economic stagnation
New constitution adopted with broad consensus—considered most progressive Arab constitution; hybrid presidential-parliamentary system
Nobel Peace Prize awarded to National Dialogue Quartet (UGTT, UTICA, Bar Association, Human Rights League) for brokering Tunisia's democratic transition
Kais Saied (anti-establishment, anti-corruption candidate) elected president; economic stagnation continues; democratic institutions weakening
July 25: Saied suspends parliament, fires prime minister, seizes emergency powers—widely described as soft coup; some initial public support due to frustration with parliament
New constitution passed by referendum (low turnout); concentrates power in presidency; effectively ends parliamentary system; opposition boycotts
Saied wins re-election (low turnout, opposition arrested); civil society space narrowing; Tunisia's democratic gains substantially reversed

lead_orgs.name: UGTT (General Union of Tunisian Workers)
lead_orgs.description: One of Africa's most powerful independent unions; provided organizational backbone for protests and National Dialogue Quartet

lead_orgs.name: LTDH (Tunisian Human Rights League)
lead_orgs.description: Oldest human rights organization in Arab world; member of Nobel Prize-winning National Dialogue Quartet

lead_orgs.name: National Dialogue Quartet (UGTT, UTICA, LTDH, Bar Association)
lead_orgs.description: Four civil society bodies that brokered democratic transition; Nobel Peace Prize 2015

funders: National Democratic Institute (NDE), IRI (US democracy promotion)
government_champions: Moncef Marzouki (first post-Ben Ali president)
Rached Ghannouchi (Ennahda leader who ultimately respected democratic norms during transition)

villains: Ben Ali family business networks ('the family')
allies: UTICA (employers' federation) joined National Dialogue Quartet

name: Egypt Arab Spring [FAILED TIP]
outcome_summary: Egypt's Arab Spring failed definitively. Mubarak fell (February 2011), democratic elections were held (2012), but military coup (July 2013) returned Egypt to authoritarianism more severe than Mubarak's. Rabaa Massacre (August 14, 2013) killed 800-1,000 Brotherhood supporters in one day. Sisi's Egypt has more political prisoners, more executions, and more surveillance than Mubarak's. This is the canonical failed-tip case: won the battle (Mubarak gone), lost the war (democracy destroyed).

progress_markers.event: June: Khaled Said beaten to death by police in Alexandria; 'We Are All Khaled Said' Facebook page (admin Wael Ghonim) accumulates 450,000 members
January 25 'Police Day': Day of Rage called following Tunisia's success; Tahrir Square occupation begins
February 11: Mubarak resigns after 18 days of protests; SCAF (military council) takes power
SCAF extends emergency law; attacks on protesters continue; virginity tests on women protesters; 'Parliament' dissolved and reconstituted
June: Mohamed Morsi (Muslim Brotherhood) elected president—first democratic election in Egypt's history
November: Morsi issues constitutional declaration granting himself near-absolute power; secular opposition turns against Brotherhood
June 30: Millions protest Morsi government; military issues 48-hour ultimatum
July 3: General Sisi leads military coup; Morsi arrested; military returns to power
August 14: Rabaa and Nahda Square massacres—security forces clear pro-Morsi sit-ins; 800-1,000 killed; Human Rights Watch calls it 'crime against humanity'
New constitution approved by referendum; Sisi 'elected' president with 96% in low-turnout election; emergency law extended
Giulio Regeni (Italian researcher documenting labor organizing) found tortured to death in Cairo; state of human rights catastrophic
Sisi 'elected' to second term; only opposition candidate allowed had previously expressed support for Sisi; political space near zero
Egypt has 60,000+ political prisoners; journalist Mohamed El-Baqer and Alaa Abd El-Fattah (imprisoned since 2011 revolution); Sisi in third term after constitutional change

lead_orgs.name: April 6 Youth Movement
lead_orgs.description: Labor solidarity movement that organized the January 25 'Day of Rage' call; modeled on Serbian Otpor movement

lead_orgs.name: We Are All Khaled Said (Facebook group)
lead_orgs.description: 450,000-member group administered by Google marketing exec Wael Ghonim; provided organizing infrastructure

lead_orgs.name: Muslim Brotherhood / Freedom and Justice Party
lead_orgs.description: Best-organized political force in Egypt; ultimately unable to navigate transition between street movement and institutional governance

funders: Muslim Brotherhood had significant organizational resources
celebrities: Wael Ghonim (Google exec, human face of revolution)
Alaa Abd El-Fattah (blogger, imprisoned across multiple presidencies)
government_champions: Mohamed El-Baradei (Nobel laureate, attempted to bridge factions)

villains: Mubarak's National Democratic Party business networks
Sisi's military-economic complex (military owns 40-60% of Egyptian economy)

international: US (ambivalent; suspended some aid briefly then restored)
Saudi Arabia and UAE (backed Sisi coup financially with $12 billion+)

name: Iran Green Movement [FAILED TIP]
outcome_summary: The Green Movement was crushed by September 2009. No electoral reforms resulted. Leaders Mousavi and Karroubi remain under house arrest as of 2025 (14+ years). The movement's networks survived underground and re-emerged in 2019 and 2022 protests. Neda Agha-Soltan's death became a global icon but could not protect the movement from state violence. Iran's political system remained fundamentally unchanged; if anything, IRGC power increased in the wake of the crackdown.

progress_markers.event: Khatami elected on reform platform; reformist decade (1997-2005) builds civil society before Ahmadinejad reversal; 'Reformist infrastructure' survives underground
Ahmadinejad wins election (disputed); reformist political space severely constrained; economic mismanagement accelerates; youth frustration builds
June 12: Official results show Ahmadinejad winning with implausible 63% margin; Mousavi and Karroubi supporters immediately dispute; 'Where is my vote?' protests begin same night
June 15: Largest protest in Iranian history since 1979; 3 million marching in Tehran in silence; eerie, disciplined, powerful
June 20: Neda Agha-Soltan shot dead on Tehran street; filmed by bystander; video transmitted globally within hours—most documented political killing in history to that point
June-September: Basij and IRGC systematically suppresses protests; Kahrizak detention center torture; opposition leaders arrested
February 11 (revolution anniversary): Final attempt at protest crushed; movement effectively over as visible force
February: Mousavi and Karroubi placed under house arrest (to present, 14+ years) after calling for sympathy protests with Arab Spring
November: Fuel price protests (40% overnight increase); regime kills 1,500+ in 4 days—shows the continuity of repressive capacity but also underground resistance
Mahsa Amini protests (Case #47) demonstrate Green Movement's legacy—same networks, same generation's children; movement was suppressed but not destroyed
Mousavi and Karroubi still under house arrest; no accountability for 2009 crackdown; Green Movement's organizational networks survive as underground infrastructure

lead_orgs.name: Mir Hossein Mousavi's campaign network
lead_orgs.description: Presidential campaign infrastructure became protest organizational backbone; decentralized but recognizable

lead_orgs.name: Reformist political networks (Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution, Islamic Iran Participation Front)
lead_orgs.description: Underground organizational infrastructure maintaining connections between protest waves

lead_orgs.name: Digital activists using early Twitter, SMS, proxy servers
lead_orgs.description: Twitter delayed maintenance for the Green Movement; proxy networks enabled communication despite internet filtering

funders: Self-funded from reform movement donor base
International human rights organizations for documentation
celebrities: Various Iranian artists and musicians in diaspora
government_champions: Mir Hossein Mousavi (Green Movement leader, imprisoned)
Mehdi Karroubi (Green Movement co-leader, imprisoned)

villains: IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps) ran crackdown and Evin Prison
Basij (voluntary militia that conducted street-level violence)
allies: Twitter (delayed maintenance to allow movement communication—minor but symbolically noted)

name: Iran Mahsa Amini Protests
outcome_summary: The protests were the largest and most sustained challenge to the Islamic Republic since 1979. The slogan 'Woman, Life, Freedom' became global. But the regime survived, and no structural political change resulted. The Guidance Patrol was briefly suspended then reinstated in new form with enhanced technology (cameras, phones). New hijab enforcement legislation (stricter) was passed in 2023. 551+ protesters were killed; 19,000+ arrested; several protesters executed. 'Woman, Life, Freedom' lives as a global frame but failed to change the Iranian state.

progress_markers.event: November: Fuel price protests; regime kills 1,500+ in 96 hours; demonstrates repressive capacity but also depth of societal anger—precondition for 2022
Summer: Hijab protests already escalating; activist Sepideh Rashno arrested for not wearing hijab; widespread solidarity even from non-political women
September 13: Mahsa (Zhina) Amini arrested by Guidance Patrol for 'improper hijab'; September 16: Dies in custody
September 16-20: Protests begin at Kasra Hospital, Amini's family outside; spread to Saqqez (her hometown) and rapidly nationwide
'Jin, Jiyan, Azadî' (Woman, Life, Freedom) in Kurdish becomes movement's global slogan—Kurdish feminist origins of the slogan honored; amplified as universal feminist frame
Female students remove hijabs in schools and universities; high school girls burning headscarves publicly; unprecedented defiance across generations
October-December: Security forces kill 551+ protesters; IRGC and Basij use live ammunition; Baloch and Kurdish protesters disproportionately targeted
Guidance Patrol (morality police) announced 'suspended' by Attorney General November 2022—never officially confirmed, operationally reduced briefly
Four protesters executed by Iran for alleged crimes committed during protests; dozens more on death row; thousands still imprisoned
New Hijab and Chastity law passed June 2023—harsher penalties than before; cars with unveiled women can be impounded; businesses can be closed
Guidance Patrol reinstated under new 'Noor' (Light) plan using CCTV cameras; AI-assisted enforcement; repression continues with technology upgrade
'Woman, Life, Freedom' lives as global feminist frame; networks inside Iran maintain quiet resistance; no structural political change; Khamenei still Supreme Leader

lead_orgs.name: No centralized leadership—deliberately leaderless
lead_orgs.description: Decentralized coordination through Signal, Telegram, VPN-accessed Instagram; leaderless structure prevented decapitation but also limited escalation strategy

lead_orgs.name: 1420 Telegram channel
lead_orgs.description: Major coordination and documentation channel with 7 million+ subscribers during peak protests

lead_orgs.name: Iran Human Rights (IHR)
lead_orgs.description: Oslo-based NGO that documented casualty counts and named victims; most reliable international source

celebrities: Massive international diaspora celebrity support (Iranian film directors, musicians, athletes)
Nazanin Boniadi (actress, became major international spokesperson)
government_champions: No domestic government champions in a theocracy

villains: IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps)
Basij militia
Telecommunications companies that cooperated with internet shutdowns

international: FIFA (Iran played World Cup during protests; team complicated relationship with movement)

name: Lebanon Thawra (October Revolution)
outcome_summary: Lebanon's October Revolution failed to produce any structural political change. PM Hariri resigned but sectarian system continued. COVID then the Beirut Port Explosion (August 4, 2020—218 killed, 300,000 homeless) further devastated the country. By 2021-2022, Lebanon had the world's highest inflation rate and 90%+ poverty rate. Political class remained unchanged; Hezbollah's political influence continued; no new constitution was drafted. The movement correctly diagnosed Lebanon's terminal kleptocracy and could not cure it.

progress_markers.event: #YouStink garbage crisis movement demonstrates mass mobilization capacity but achieves only limited concessions; establishes civil society activist generation
Economic crisis worsens through 2019; electricity cuts 20 hours/day in some areas; bank dollar withdrawals restricted; government hasn't passed budget in 10 years
October 17: WhatsApp tax announcement ($0.20/day) + government failure to fight Shouf forest fire triggers spontaneous street explosions simultaneously across the country
Human chain across entire country—first time in Lebanese history citizens physically connected from Tripoli to Tyre despite sectarian geography
October 29: PM Hariri resigns—first visible concession from political class
'Kellun ya3ne kellun' (All of them means all of them) becomes movement's demand for complete political class replacement; unprecedented cross-sectarian solidarity
Hezbollah and Amal supporters attack protest camps in Beirut; sectarian factions try to contain or discredit movement
January: Banks continue restricting withdrawals; economic collapse accelerates; COVID lockdowns suppress protests in March-April
August 4: Beirut Port Explosion—2,750 tons of ammonium nitrate stored unsafely for 6 years; 218 killed; 7,000 wounded; 300,000 homeless
Post-explosion rage briefly revives protests but political class survives; Hassan Diab government resigns; no accountability for explosion
Lebanese pound loses 90%+ of value; bank accounts effectively wiped out; poverty rate reaches 78%; World Bank describes as 'deliberate depression'
Judge Tarek Bitar's Beirut blast investigation repeatedly sabotaged; politicians face immunity claims; Lebanon remains in political stalemate
