Joseph Aoun elected president January 2025 (after 2-year vacuum)—very small progress; sectarian system essentially unchanged; no accountability for explosion or economic collapse

lead_orgs.name: No formal organization—explicitly leaderless
lead_orgs.description: Rejection of political parties and NGOs as compromised; civil society groups like LiHaqqi and Minteshreen coordinated tactically

lead_orgs.name: LiHaqqi (For My Rights)
lead_orgs.description: Electoral reform organization that tried to convert Thawra energy into 2022 election campaigns; had limited success

lead_orgs.name: Lawyers' syndicate and professional unions
lead_orgs.description: Provided some organizational infrastructure; staged their own strikes

funders: Self-funded; some diaspora support for protest logistics
government_champions: Judge Tarek Bitar (Beirut blast investigator, repeatedly targeted)
A handful of reform-minded MPs

villains: Lebanese banking sector (Riad Salameh, central bank governor, under investigation for $330 million fraud)
Fuel importers (cartel operating amid subsidies)
Electricity holding companies with political connections

international: France (Macron flew to Beirut post-explosion; CEDRE conference funding)
IMF (negotiations deadlocked due to political obstruction)
World Bank (published devastating assessment of Lebanon's 'deliberate depression')

name: Israel Judicial Reform Protests
outcome_summary: The protests successfully delayed the judicial overhaul for approximately 9 months—a tactical win. The first reform element (eliminating the 'reasonableness' standard) was passed in July 2023 but struck down by the Supreme Court in January 2024 (13-0 vote). October 7, 2023 Hamas attacks suspended the protest movement as the country went to war. By 2025, the Netanyahu government is pursuing judicial reform through different mechanisms under cover of wartime. The democratic crisis was delayed, not resolved.

progress_markers.event: November: Netanyahu returns to power with most right-wing coalition in Israeli history; Itamar Ben-Gvir and Bezalel Smotrich in cabinet; judicial overhaul announced
January 7: Judicial overhaul package announced; first protests begin; legal experts warn of fundamental damage to democratic checks
January-March: Saturday night protests grow from tens of thousands to 100,000+ weekly; 'brothers and sisters' framing; IDF reserve pilots announce strike
March 26: Netanyahu fires Defense Minister Gallant who warned judicial overhaul threatening military readiness; 400,000 protest Ayalon Highway in spontaneous response
March 27: Netanyahu announces 'pause' of judicial overhaul—first major concession; business sector, defense community pressure was decisive
July: Knesset passes first element of overhaul (eliminating 'reasonableness' standard) despite resumed protests; Netanyahu resumes package
October 7: Hamas attacks; 1,200 killed; 250 hostages taken; country goes to war; protest movement suspended
November: War cabinet formed; emergency coalition dilutes far-right influence temporarily
January: Supreme Court strikes down reasonableness law 13-0; Netanyahu government refuses to comply—constitutional crisis
Judicial reform pursuit continues through other legislative tracks; Netanyahu under ICC warrant; protest movement partially resumes around hostage deal and democratic concerns
Democratic crisis ongoing; government replacing attorney generals and state prosecutors; judicial independence under sustained attack; wartime conditions suppress full protest revival

lead_orgs.name: Brothers in Arms (Achim L'Neshek)
lead_orgs.description: Military reserve soldiers' organization; their refusal to serve drill threat was the single most powerful leverage point of the movement

lead_orgs.name: Kaplan Force
lead_orgs.description: Tel Aviv-centered protest organization that coordinated the mass weekly demonstrations

lead_orgs.name: UnXeptable
lead_orgs.description: High-tech sector protest group; organized tech industry opposition including threats to move capital offshore

government_champions: Yair Lapid (opposition leader)
Benny Gantz (joined war cabinet, later left)
Yoav Gallant (Defense Minister who was fired for speaking out)

villains: Netanyahu government and coalition partners (Likud, Religious Zionist Party, Otzma Yehudit)
Yariv Levin (Justice Minister, architect of overhaul)
allies: High-tech sector (collectively warned of capital flight)
Bank of Israel (warned of economic consequences)

international: German, British, French foreign ministers made statements
American Jewish community (deeply divided; liberal orgs opposed reform)

name: Jordan Bread Protests
outcome_summary: The protests achieved their immediate demand—PM Hani Mulki resigned and the tax bill was withdrawn for revision. But Jordan's structural economic problems remain: IMF conditions continue, youth unemployment persists, and inequality has not been addressed. The withdrawal was tactical; a modified tax law was subsequently passed. The Jordan protests demonstrate how economic-shock protests can extract concessions from regimes that maintain legitimacy through material distribution, without producing structural reform. The professional unions' cross-class mobilization was a genuine innovation.

progress_markers.event: Arab Spring reaches Jordan; modest protests; King Abdullah announces reform program; some constitutional amendments; protest movement contained through modest concessions
Jordan negotiates 3-year IMF Extended Fund Facility ($723 million); austerity measures begin; fuel and food subsidies reduced; cost of living rising
April-May: IMF pushes income tax law reform as condition for continued support; draft law would double tax rates for middle class; professional unions organize opposition
May 30: General strike by 33 trade unions; June 1: Midnight fuel price increase triggers immediate street protests
June 1-4: Unprecedented scenes—lawyers, doctors, engineers leading protests alongside workers and poor; 'We want to bring down the government' chant in Amman
June 4: PM Hani Mulki resigns; Omar Razzaz appointed; Razzaz announces intention to withdraw income tax bill
June 7: King Abdullah accepts withdrawal of income tax bill for further consultation; protests cease; movement wins tactical demand
Modified income tax law passed with some revisions; IMF structural adjustment continues; underlying conditions unchanged
COVID-19 economic shock compounds austerity; IMF provides additional support with further conditionality; Jordan's debt-to-GDP exceeds 110%
Professional unions continue to be major political voice; teachers' union protests 2022 suppressed; some gains in civil society space
Jordan's economy dependent on Gulf aid and US/IMF support; structural reform stalled; professional unions remain best-organized civil society sector

lead_orgs.name: Professional Associations Council (PAC)
lead_orgs.description: Umbrella coordinating body for Jordan's 14 professional unions (lawyers, doctors, engineers, pharmacists, etc.)—strongest civil society institutional force in Jordan

lead_orgs.name: General Federation of Jordanian Trade Unions (GFJTU)
lead_orgs.description: National labor federation that called the general strike and provided labor protest infrastructure

lead_orgs.name: Hirak (Popular Movement)
lead_orgs.description: Youth movement networks that coordinated street protests; emerged from 2011 Arab Spring organizing

funders: Professional union membership dues funded organizing
celebrities: Various Jordanian public intellectuals and journalists
government_champions: Omar Razzaz (incoming PM who agreed to withdraw bill)

villains: IMF (external conditionality driving austerity)
Gulf state creditors who supported the IMF program

international: IMF (paradoxically both the cause of protests and the institution Jordan must negotiate with)
Gulf Cooperation Council (Saudi Arabia, UAE—major donors)
